html:
Number 34

On their right as they entered, between the main door and the inner one, was the porterвАЩs lodge. An official of the French type, in the grey livery of the man at the station, was sitting at the telephone, reading the newspaper. He came out and led them through the well-lighted halls, on the left of which lay the reception-rooms. Hans Castorp peered in as he passed, but they were empty. Where, then, were the guests, he asked, and his cousin answered: вАЬIn the rest-cure. I had leave tonight to go out and meet you. Otherwise I am always up in my balcony, after supper.вАЭ

Hans Castorp came near bursting out again. вАЬWhat! You lie out on your balcony at night, in the damp?вАЭ he asked, his voice shaking.

вАЬYes, that is the rule. From eight to ten. But come and see your room now, and get a wash.вАЭ

They entered the liftвБ†вАФit was an electric one, worked by the Frenchman. As they went up, Hans Castorp wiped his eyes.

вАЬIвАЩm perfectly worn out with laughing,вАЭ he said, and breathed through his mouth. вАЬYouвАЩve told me such a lot of crazy stuffвБ†вАФthat about the psychoanalysis was the last straw. I suppose I am a bit relaxed from the journey. And my feet are coldвБ†вАФare yours? But my face burns so, it is really unpleasant. Do we eat now? I feel hungry. Is the food decent up here?вАЭ

They went noiselessly along the coco matting of the narrow corridor, which was lighted by electric lights in white glass shades set in the ceiling. The walls gleamed with hard white enamel paint. They had a glimpse of a nursing sister in a white cap, and eyeglasses on a cord that ran behind her ear. She had the look of a Protestant sisterвБ†вАФthat is to say, one working without a real vocation and burdened with restlessness and ennui. As they went along the corridor, Hans Castorp saw, beside two of the white-enamelled, numbered doors, certain curious, swollen-looking, balloon-shaped vessels with short necks. He did not think, at the moment, to ask what they were.

вАЬHere you are,вАЭ said Joachim. вАЬI am next you on the right. The other side you have a Russian couple, rather loud and offensive, but it couldnвАЩt be helped. Well, how do you like it?вАЭ

There were two doors, an outer and an inner, with clothes-hooks in the space between. Joachim had turned on the ceiling light, and in its vibrating brilliance the room looked restful and cheery, with practical white furniture, white washable walls, clean linoleum, and white linen curtains gaily embroidered in modern taste. The door stood open; one saw the lights of the valley and heard distant dance-music. The good Joachim had put a vase of flowers on the chest of drawersвБ†вАФa few bluebells and some yarrow, which he had found himself among the second crop of grass on the slopes.

вАЬAwfully decent of you,вАЭ said Hans Castorp. вАЬWhat a nice room! I can spend a couple of weeks here with pleasure.вАЭ

вАЬAn American woman died here day before yesterday,вАЭ said Joachim. вАЬBehrens told me directly that she would be out before you came, and you might have the room. Her fianc√© was with her, an English officer of marines, but he didnвАЩt behave very well. He kept coming out in the corridor to cry, just like a little boy. He rubbed cold cream on his cheeks, because he was close-shaven and the tears smarted. Night before last she had two first-class haemorrhages, and that was the finish. But she has been gone since yesterday morning, and after they took her away of course they fumigated the room thoroughly with formalin, which is the proper thing to use in such cases.вАЭ

Hans Castorp took in this information with a sprightly, yet half-distraught air. He was standing with his sleeves pushed back before the roomy wash-hand-basin, the taps of which shone in the electric light, and gave hardly a glance at the white metal bed with its fresh coverlet.

вАЬFumigated it, eh? ThatвАЩs ripping,вАЭ he said loquaciously and rather absurdly, as he washed and dried his hands. вАЬMethyl aldehyde; yes, thatвАЩs too much for the bacteria, no matter how strong they are. H2CO. But itвАЩs a powerful stench. Of course, perfect sanitation is absolutely essential.вАЭ He spoke with more of a Hamburg accent than his cousin, who had broken himself of it since his student days. Hans Castorp continued volubly. вАЬBut what I was about to say was, probably the officer of marines used a safety-razor; one makes oneself sore with those things easier than with a well-sharpened bladeвБ†вАФat least, that is my experience, and I use them both by turns. Well, and salt water would naturally make a tender skin smart, so he got in the way, in the service, of rubbing in cold cream. I donвАЩt see anything strange about thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He rattled on: said that he had two hundred Maria Mancinis (his cigar) in his trunk, the customs officers had been very courteous; and gave his cousin greetings from various people at home. вАЬDonвАЩt they heat the rooms here?вАЭ he broke off to inquire, and ran to put his hands on the radiator.

вАЬNo, they keep us pretty cool,вАЭ answered Joachim. вАЬThe weather would have to be different from this before they put on the heat in August.вАЭ

вАЬAugust, August!вАЭ said Hans Castorp. вАЬBut I am cold, abominably cold; I mean in my body, for my face burns shockinglyвБ†вАФjust feel it!вАЭ

This demand was entirely foreign to the young manвАЩs natureвБ†вАФso much so that he himself was disagreeably impressed as he heard himself make it. Joachim did not take up the offer, but merely said: вАЬThat is the airвБ†вАФit doesnвАЩt mean anything; Behrens himself is purple in the face all day long. Some people never get used to it. Come along now, do, or we shanвАЩt get anything to eat.вАЭ

Outside they saw the nursing sister again, peering short-sightedly and inquisitively after them. But in the first storey Hans Castorp suddenly stopped, rooted to the spot by a perfectly ghastly sound coming from a little distance off round a bend in the corridor. It was not a loud sound, but so distinctly horrible that Hans Castorp made a wry face and looked wide-eyed at his cousin. It was coughing, obviously, a man coughing; but coughing like to no other Hans Castorp had ever heard, and compared with which any other had been a magnificent and healthy manifestation of life: a coughing that had no conviction and gave no relief, that did not even come out in paroxysms, but was just a feeble, dreadful welling up of the juices of organic dissolution.

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Joachim. вАЬThatвАЩs a bad case. An Austrian aristocrat, you know, very elegant. HeвАЩs a born horsemanвБ†вАФa gentleman rider. And now heвАЩs come to this. But he still gets about.вАЭ

As they went, Hans Castorp discoursed earnestly upon the gentleman riderвАЩs cough.

вАЬYou must realize,вАЭ he said, вАЬthat IвАЩve never heard anything like it before. It is entirely new to me, and naturally it makes a great impression. There are different kinds of cough, dry and loose, and people always say the loose one is better than the other, the barking kind. When I had croup, in my youthвАЭ (he actually said вАЬin my youthвАЭ!), вАЬI bayed like a wolf, and I can still remember how glad everybody was when it got looser. But a cough like thisвБ†вАФI didnвАЩt know there was such a cough! It isnвАЩt a human cough at all. It isnвАЩt dry and yet isnвАЩt loose eitherвБ†вАФthat is very far from being the right word for it. It is just as if one could look right into him when he coughs, and see what it looks like: all slime and mucousвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬOh,вАЭ said Joachim, вАЬI hear it every day, you donвАЩt need to describe it to me.вАЭ

But Hans Castorp could not get over the coughing he had heard. He kept repeating that he could see right into the gentleman riderвАЩs vitals; when they reached the restaurant his travel-weary eyes had an excited glitter.
